description: Flavour symptom → extraction diagnosis → per-method fixes. Grounded in James Hoffmann transcripts + SCA extraction science.
sour_bitter_note: IMPORTANT: Sour and bitter are often confused. Sour = acidity felt on sides of tongue, like green apple or lemon. Bitter = harsh finish that fills the mouth, like dark chocolate or tonic water. Misidentifying means fixing the wrong problem and making it worse.

flavour_symptoms.describes: Unpleasant tingling on the sides of the tongue; tastes like lemon juice or unripe fruit. Acidity with no sweetness to balance it.
flavour_symptoms.cause_explanation: Not enough soluble compounds have dissolved. The pleasant sugars and balancing compounds extract later than acids. Short contact time or low temp means only early-extracting acids make it into the cup.
flavour_symptoms.confusion_warning: Often mislabelled as 'bitter'. If the sharpness is on the sides of your tongue and the coffee tastes thin, it's sour. If it's a heavy finish at the back of the mouth, it's bitter.
universal_fixes: Grind finer to increase surface area and slow flow rate
Use more coffee relative to water (adjust ratio)

v60: Pour slower to extend contact time
Try a longer bloom (45s instead of 30s)
french_press: Grind slightly finer (counter to traditional advice)
Extend steep time from 4 to 5–6 minutes
Use just-boiled water, don't let it cool
espresso: Grind finer — this is your primary lever
moka_pot: Keep heat low and steady to slow extraction
Don't let it sputter — remove from heat as soon as flow slows

flavour_symptoms.describes: Unpleasant, heavy finish that fills the back of the mouth. Like over-steeped tea, burnt toast, or tonic water. Lingers uncomfortably.
flavour_symptoms.cause_explanation: Too many compounds have dissolved, including the late-extracting harsh phenols and woody compounds. Also triggered by dirty equipment, dark roast, or excessively hot water.
flavour_symptoms.confusion_warning: Bitterness felt in the finish vs sourness felt on the tongue sides. A little bitterness is natural and desirable — it becomes a problem when it dominates.
other_causes: Dark roast — inherently more bitter regardless of extraction
Dirty machine — old oils go rancid and add harsh bitterness
Very hard water — high bicarbonate amplifies bitter notes

v60: Pour faster or reduce total pour time
Use water at 90–93°C for darker roasts
french_press: Skim the crust and remove floating grounds before decanting
Decant immediately — don't leave coffee sitting on grounds
espresso: Clean portafilter, basket, and group head
Try a medium roast instead of dark
moka_pot: Reduce heat — high temperature extracts harsh compounds
Remove from heat earlier, before the final sputtering

flavour_symptoms.describes: Coffee tastes like dirty water. No body, no weight on the palate. Feels like you're drinking very faint coffee.
flavour_symptoms.cause_explanation: Not enough dissolved coffee solids in the cup. Either too little coffee was used relative to water (ratio problem) or grind was too coarse and water passed through too quickly.
universal_fixes: Grind finer to slow flow and increase extraction
Check your scale — you may be using less coffee than you think

v60: Pour in smaller pulses to keep water contact time up
aeropress: Use less water for a more concentrated result, then dilute if needed
espresso: Grind finer to slow the shot
moka_pot: Fill the basket fully and level
Don't tamp — just level the grounds
Ensure a tight seal on the brewer
chemex: Use more coffee — at least 1:15 ratio

flavour_symptoms.describes: Coffee has no brightness, no sweetness, no life. Everything tastes muted. Not offensive, just boring and one-dimensional.
flavour_symptoms.cause_explanation: Sweetness and complexity come from volatile aromatics and sugars — these are destroyed by staleness (CO2 off-gassing carrying away aromas) or under-extraction. Can also indicate the wrong bean for your taste.
other_causes: Stale coffee — most common cause; use coffee within 2–6 weeks of roast date
Pre-ground coffee loses aromatics within minutes
Robusta or low-quality beans lack sweetness potential
Brewing too cold — sweetness compounds need heat to extract
universal_fixes: Check roast date — switch to fresher beans first
Try a slight grind adjustment finer to increase extraction

v60: Ensure bloom is vigorous — fresh coffee should bubble
espresso: Ensure machine is at full temperature before brewing
moka_pot: Medium roast tends to show more sweetness than dark

flavour_symptoms.describes: Unpleasant sediment or sludge in the cup. Texture feels sandy or gritty. Cup looks cloudy.
flavour_symptoms.cause_explanation: Fine coffee particles (fines) have made it into the cup, either due to a coarse grind producing many fines, a worn grinder burr, or incorrect brewing technique with metal filters or a plunger.
universal_fixes: Ensure filter paper is properly seated and rinsed
Don't disturb settled grounds before decanting
Check grinder for worn burrs producing inconsistent particle size

v60: Rinse paper filter thoroughly before adding coffee
french_press: After 4 min steep, skim and wait 5–8 more minutes for grounds to settle
Push plunger only to surface — do not press all the way down
Pour gently, stop before the last 10% which holds all sediment
aeropress: Use two paper filters for cleaner cup
Press slowly and stop before you hear hissing
espresso: Not typically an issue — the basket filters fines
moka_pot: Don't grind too fine or fines block the filter plate
chemex: Paper filter should fully cover the bottom and sides of the funnel

flavour_symptoms.describes: A dry, papery or cardboard-like taste. Like drinking weak tea made with old teabags. Sometimes described as wooden or dusty.
flavour_symptoms.cause_explanation: Most commonly caused by unrinsed paper filters leaching paper flavour. Also caused by stale coffee that has lost all volatiles, or over-roasted coffee.
universal_fixes: Always rinse paper filters with hot water before adding coffee and discard rinse water
Try different roaster — some papers taste more neutral than others

v60: Rinse filter for 10+ seconds before brewing
french_press: No paper filter — if you get this taste, it's stale coffee
espresso: Not a paper issue — check coffee age and machine cleanliness
moka_pot: No filter paper used — check coffee freshness
chemex: Thick Chemex filters especially need thorough rinsing — 20–30 seconds

flavour_symptoms.symptom: Hollow / No Body / Bright but Empty
flavour_symptoms.describes: Interesting acidity and some flavour but no weight, no mouthfeel, no aftertaste. The coffee feels thin despite tasting okay initially.
flavour_symptoms.cause_explanation: Body and mouthfeel come from oils, proteins and later-extracting compounds. If extraction is slightly short, you get acidity first but body doesn't develop. Often happens with very light roasts or coarse grinds.
universal_fixes: Try a slightly lower temperature to slow extraction and allow body compounds more time

v60: Reduce agitation — swirling increases clarity but reduces body
french_press: French press naturally adds body via metal filter — ensure full steep time
aeropress: Use metal filter instead of paper for more body
moka_pot: Ensure full extraction — don't remove from heat too early
chemex: Chemex filters remove oils — some body loss is natural; try a slightly finer grind
